questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Activity selection classic greedy picks:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The compatible activity that finishes earliest
questions.explanation: Earliest finish leaves max room for future activities.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Fractional knapsack is greedy by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Value/weight density; take fraction of the last item
questions.explanation: Fractions allowed ⇒ density sort works.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why does 0/1 knapsack make pure density greedy fail? Which response is most accurate?
options: You cannot take a fraction — a high-density item may block a better combo
questions.explanation: Indivisibility breaks the fractional argument.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: An exchange argument shows greedy correctness by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Transforming any optimal solution into the greedy one without worsening
questions.explanation: High-level proof pattern for greedy.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Huffman coding builds codes by repeatedly:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Merging two least frequent nodes into a parent
questions.explanation: Greedy for optimal prefix codes under symbol frequencies.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A greedy algorithm makes a sequence of choices that are:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Locally best according to a rule, hoping for global optimum
questions.explanation: Need a proof or known theorem for guarantees.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Interval scheduling to maximize number of jobs uses:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Same family as activity selection.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: When greedy fails, a typical symptom is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A counterexample where a locally best choice ruins the global optimum
questions.explanation: Always hunt counterexamples in interviews.

questions.stem: Coin change greedy (largest coin first) works for canonical systems like US coins but:
options: Fails for some denomination sets — need DP
questions.explanation: Classic interview caveat. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Minimum number of platforms / meeting rooms relates to greedy by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Tracking overlaps via sorting starts/ends or a heap of ends
questions.explanation: Sweep line / heap of end times.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Jump game I (reach last index) greedy tracks:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Update maxReach; fail if i > maxReach.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Assign cookies / soft greedy often sorts and:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Matches smallest sufficient cookie to greediest remaining child factor
questions.explanation: Sort both arrays; two pointers.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Gas station circular tour greedy idea resets start when:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Tank goes negative; choose next index as new start
questions.explanation: If total gas≥cost, unique start exists under classic constraints.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Scheduling unit-time tasks with deadlines/penalties often uses:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Greedy by profit with slot finding / disjoint sets
questions.explanation: Classic greedy scheduling variant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Prim and Dijkstra are 'greedy' in the sense that they:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Commit to the best local edge/node choice under a proven invariant
questions.explanation: Greedy algorithms with cut/shortest-path proofs.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Kruskal is greedy because it:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Always considers the next lightest edge globally
questions.explanation: Greedy MST via sorted edges. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A safe greedy choice property means:? Choose the strongest option.
options: There exists an optimal solution consistent with the greedy first choice
questions.explanation: Part of the formal greedy framework.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Candy distribution (ratings) two-pass greedy ensures:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Higher rating gets more than neighbors by scanning both directions
questions.explanation: Left-to-right then right-to-left max.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Boats to save people (weight limit) often pairs:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Two pointers after sort. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Greedy vs DP one-liner:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Greedy commits without revisiting; DP compares multiple subproblem options
questions.explanation: Conceptual distinction for interviews.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Fractional knapsack proof sketch relies on:? Choose the strongest option.
options: If an optimal solution differs, exchange toward higher density without loss
questions.explanation: Exchange/density argument. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: Activity selection proof: if greedy picks a and OPT picks b finishing later,
options: Replace b with a; feasibility preserved, count not worse
questions.explanation: Classic exchange. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Huffman optimality needs:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Prefix-free codes and greedy merge of least frequent symbols
questions.explanation: Kraft inequality / exchange intuitions in textbooks.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Minimum spanning tree greedy works due to:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Same foundation as Prim/Kruskal.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Job sequencing with deadlines: sort by profit then:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Place each job in the latest free slot before deadline
questions.explanation: Greedy placement pattern. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Non-overlapping intervals to remove minimum intervals: greedy by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Earliest end time keep; count removals of overlaps
questions.explanation: Equivalent to max activities kept.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Queue reconstruction by height uses greedy insert because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Place tallest first so k-count inserts are stable
questions.explanation: Sort by height desc, k asc; insert at index k.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Minimum arrows to burst balloons sorts by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: End coordinate; shoot at end; skip overlapping starts
questions.explanation: Greedy stabbing intervals. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: When a problem asks for lexicographically smallest subsequence with constraints, greedy often uses a:
options: Monotonic stack discarding larger letters when frequency remains
questions.explanation: Stack + remaining counts pattern.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Huffman tree height relates to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Skewed frequencies → longer codes for rare symbols
questions.explanation: More frequent ⇒ shorter depth typically.

questions.stem: Greedy coloring of interval graphs (register allocation intuition) sorts by:
options: Start times and assigns lowest available color/room
questions.explanation: Interval graphs are perfect for greedy coloring.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Matroids (senior-lite): greedy works on structures where:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Independent sets satisfy exchange axioms allowing weight-greedy
questions.explanation: Abstract reason many greedy algorithms succeed.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Minimize max load / assign tasks: greedy might:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Always put next job on current least loaded worker (with caveats)
questions.explanation: List scheduling; approximation context in advanced talks.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Stamping / covering problems fail naive greedy when:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A locally maximal stamp blocks a needed global cover
questions.explanation: Build counterexamples. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Two-pointer greedy after sorting often needs the array to be:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Sorted so extremes move inward correctly
questions.explanation: Sorting enables the greedy pairing.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Dijkstra as greedy: the settled node's distance is final because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: With non-negative weights, no path through unsettled can improve it
questions.explanation: Key invariant. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: Activity selection variant maximizing total weight of intervals generally needs:
options: DP / weighted interval scheduling, not plain earliest-finish greedy
questions.explanation: Weights break the unweighted greedy.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Egyptian fractions / greedy Fibonacci coding style can:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Loop on largest unit fraction ≤ remainder — sometimes suboptimal historically
questions.explanation: Historical greedy; know limitations.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Sliding window maximum via deque is greedy-ish in that it:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Discards indices that can never be the max later
questions.explanation: Monotonic deque maintains candidates.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Interview answer structure for greedy:? Choose the strongest option.
options: State rule → show safe choice / exchange → complexity; else give counterexample & DP
questions.explanation: How seniors talk through greedy.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Weighted interval scheduling optimal algorithm is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Sort by finish; DP with p(j) predecessor binary search — not pure greedy
questions.explanation: Classic CLRS/Kleinberg shift from greedy to DP.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Approximation: set cover greedy by max new elements gives:? Choose the strongest option.
options: H_n ≈ ln n approximation in general
questions.explanation: Famous greedy approximation bound.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A counterexample for coin greedy on denominations {1,3,4} amount 6:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Standard teaching counterexample.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Submodular maximization with greedy (k-set) yields:? Choose the strongest option.
options: (1-1/e) approximation for monotone submodular under cardinality
questions.explanation: ML/interview theory crossover.

questions.stem: Why earliest-deadline-first scheduling is optimal for feasible unit jobs on one machine:
options: Exchange argument swapping inversions of deadlines
questions.explanation: Classic scheduling greedy. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Online greedy (ski rental, caching) differs from offline because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Future input unknown — competitive ratio replaces optimality
questions.explanation: Online algorithms framing. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Kruskal with almost-sorted edges still needs Union-Find because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: You must skip cycle-forming edges even if nearly sorted
questions.explanation: Greedy choice still constrained by acyclicity.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Senior bug: 'greedy by start time' for max activities. Failure mode? Which response is most accurate?
options: A long early-starting activity blocks many short ones
questions.explanation: Wrong criterion ⇒ counterexample.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Huffman vs Shannon codes interview point:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Huffman optimal among prefix codes for known frequencies; Shannon is a different construction bound
questions.explanation: Know what optimality means. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: When pressed 'is greedy correct?', best senior move is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Produce a short invariant/exchange proof or a minimal counterexample
questions.explanation: Proof or counterexample — no middle ground.
